steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
I just made some changes to my personal site and checked out master, but now I can't find those changes. Please help me find them and merge them into master.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@638b5db054c4:/app/personal-site#

Analysis: I'm in the /app/personal-site directory and the user is on the master branch. They mention they made changes to their personal site but can't find them after checking out master. This suggests the changes might be in a different branch or in the stash.
Plan: First, I'll check the current branch status and see what branches exist. Then I'll check if there are any stashed changes. I'll also look at the git log to understand the current state of the repository.

root@638b5db054c4:/app/personal-site# git stash list
root@638b5db054c4:/app/personal-site# git log --oneline -5
d7d3e4b (HEAD -> master) off the job market woo
c4e38a1 Add code link for BLADE
6f46fa0 CV Update
dd7e395 Adds BLADE to bib
2cd355a n+1 (for the last time???)
root@638b5db054c4:/app/personal-site#

steps.message:
Analysis: I can see that the user is on the master branch with 5 commits visible. There are no branches shown (only master) and no stashed changes. The user mentioned they made changes but can't find them after checking out master. Let me check if there are any uncommitted changes or if there are other branches that might not be listed.
Plan: I'll check for uncommitted changes with git status, and also look at all refs including dangling commits that might contain the user's changes. Git reflog can help find recently referenced commits that may no longer be on any branch.

650dba4 HEAD@{1}: commit: Move to Stanford
c4e38a1 HEAD@{2}: checkout: moving from master to HEAD~1
d7d3e4b (HEAD -> master) HEAD@{3}: reset: moving to d7d3e4b
b0d59cd HEAD@{4}: clone: from https://github.com/TheMikeMerrill/personal-site.git
root@638b5db054c4:/app/personal-site#

steps.message:
Analysis: The reflog shows interesting history. There's a commit '650dba4 HEAD@{1}: commit: Move to Stanford' which is not currently on the master branch. This commit was made from master but then the user checked out master again. The commit at HEAD@{1} (650dba4) appears to be the user's changes that are no longer on any branch. This is a dangling commit.
Plan: I'll examine the commit 650dba4 to see if it contains the changes the user made. If it's the right one, I'll merge it into master. First, let me look at what that commit contains.

steps.message:
Analysis: The commit 650dba4 contains the changes the user made - updating their status from 'PhD Student at UW' to 'Postdoctoral Researcher at Stanford'. This commit is not currently on the master branch. I can see the changes include updates to _includes/about.md and _layouts/default.html.
Plan: Now I'll merge commit 650dba4 into master. I'll use git cherry-pick to apply this commit to the current master branch since it's a single commit that needs to be merged.

Plan: Let me check the current state of the file with the conflict to understand what needs to be resolved, then resolve it appropriately.

steps.message:
Analysis: I can see the merge conflict. The HEAD (current master) has 'I am a sixth PhD candidate at the Paul G. Allen School...' while the commit 650dba4 has 'I am a Postdoctoral Researcher at Stanford CS.' The user wants to merge their changes (the Stanford update), so I need to resolve this by keeping the Stanford version.
Plan: I'll resolve the conflict by accepting the changes from the commit (the Stanford version) and then continue the cherry-pick. I'll write the resolved content to the file.

steps.message:
Analysis: The cherry-pick is waiting for an editor to edit the commit message, but there's no editor available. I need to either set a default editor or use the -m flag to provide a commit message directly.
Plan: I'll abort the current cherry-pick and redo it with a commit message provided via -m flag to avoid needing an editor.

error: option `mainline' expects a number greater than zero
root@638b5db054c4:/app/personal-site#

steps.message:
Analysis: The -m flag in git cherry-pick is used for merge commits to specify which parent to use as the mainline. For a regular commit like 650dba4, I need to use --no-ff or just provide the message differently. Let me try using --no-commit or setting up a simple editor.
Plan: I'll try cherry-pick without the -m flag but use GIT_EDITOR to specify a simple editor, or use the --no-commit flag to apply the changes without committing, then commit manually with a message.
